name: Mock Test - 04
instruction: The Question Paper contains three sections. Section A has 25 questions. Attempt any 20 questions. Section B has 24 questions. Attempt any20 questions. Section C has 6 questions. Attempt any 5 questions. All questions carry equal marks. There is no negative marking.

questions.question_text: If the amounts of electric flux entering and leaving an enclosed surface respectively are {tex}\phi_1{/tex} and {tex}\phi_2{/tex} , the electric charge inside the surface will be :
questions.question_html: If the amounts of electric flux entering and leaving an enclosed surface respectively are {tex}\phi_1{/tex} and {tex}\phi_2{/tex}, the electric charge inside the surface will be :
questions.explanation: The net electric flux leaving the enclosed surface = {tex}\left(\phi_{2}-\phi_{1}\right){/tex} If q is net charge inside the enclosed surface, then according to Gauss' theorem, {tex}\phi_{2}-\phi_{1}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} or {tex}q=\left(\phi_{2}-\phi_{1}\right) \varepsilon_{0}{/tex}

questions.question_text: A series combination of n 1 capacitors, each of value C 1 is charged by a source of potential difference 4V. When another parallel combination of n 2 capacitors, each of value C 2 , is charged by a source of potential difference V, it has the same (total) energy stored in it, as the first combination has. The value of C 2 , in terms of C 1 , is then:
questions.question_html: A series combination of n1 capacitors, each of value C1 is charged by a source of potential difference 4V. When another parallel combination of n2 capacitors, each of value C2, is charged by a source of potential difference V, it has the same (total) energy stored in it, as the first combination has. The value of C2, in terms of C1, is then:

questions.question_text: The mobility of charge carriers increases with:
questions.question_html: The mobility of charge carriers increases with:

options.text: increase in the average collision time
options.html: increase in the average collision time

options.text: increase in the mass of the charge carriers
options.html: increase in the mass of the charge carriers

options.text: the decrease in the charge of the mobile carriers
options.html: the decrease in the charge of the mobile carriers

questions.correct_option_text: increase in the average collision time
questions.explanation: Mobility, {tex}\mu=\frac{q \tau}{m} \text { i.e., } \mu \propto \tau{/tex}

questions.question_text: In Fig, two positive charges q 2 and q 3 fixed along the y axis, exert a net electric force in the + x direction on a charge q 1 fixed along the x axis. If a positive charge Q is added at (x, 0), the force on q 1
questions.question_html: In Fig, two positive charges q2 and q3 fixed along the y axis, exert a net electric force in the + x direction on a charge q1 fixed along the x axis. If a positive charge Q is added at (x, 0), the force on q1

options.text: shall point along the negative x-axis.
options.html: shall point along the negative x-axis.

options.text: shall increase along the positive x-axis.
options.html: shall increase along the positive x-axis.

options.text: shall decrease along the positive x-axis.
options.html: shall decrease along the positive x-axis.

options.text: shall increase but the direction changes because of the intersection of Q with q 2 and q 3 .
options.html: shall increase but the direction changes because of the intersection of Q with q<sub>2</sub> and q<sub>3</sub>.

questions.correct_option_text: shall increase along the positive x-axis.
questions.explanation: The total force acting on a given charge is given by the vector sum of individual forces acting on that charges.Net force on charge q 1 , by other charges q 2 and q 3 is along the + x-direction, so nature of force between q 1 and q 2 and q 1 and q 3 is attractive. This is possible when charge q 1 is negative. Now, if a positive charge Q is placed at (x, 0), then, the force on q 1 shall increase. The direction will be along the positive x-axis.

questions.question_text: Capacitance (in F) of a spherical conductor of radius 1 m is
questions.question_html: Capacitance (in F) of a spherical conductor of radius 1 m is
questions.explanation: Here, r = 1 m Now, C = {tex}4 \ \pi \ \varepsilon_0 \ r{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{9\times10^9}\times1=1\cdot11\times10^{-10} F{/tex}

questions.question_text: In the given diagram, the reading of the ammeter (when the internal resistance of the battery is zero) is:
questions.question_html: In the given diagram, the reading of the ammeter (when the internal resistance of the battery is zero) is:
questions.explanation: Reading of the ammeter = Current through 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} resistor = {tex}\frac{10 \mathrm{~V}}{5 \Omega}{/tex} = 2 A

questions.question_text: Two ends of a horizontal conducting rod of length l are joined to a voltmeter. The whole arrangement moves with a horizontal velocity v, the direction of motion being perpendicular to the rod. Vertical component of the earth's magnetic field is B. The voltmeter reads
questions.question_html: Two ends of a horizontal conducting rod of length l are joined to a voltmeter. The whole arrangement moves with a horizontal velocity v, the direction of motion being perpendicular to the rod. Vertical component of the earth's magnetic field is B. The voltmeter reads

options.text: Blv only if the rod moves eastward
options.html: Blv only if the rod moves eastward

options.text: Blv if the rod moves in any direction
options.html: Blv if the rod moves in any direction

options.text: Blv only if the rod moves westward
options.html: Blv only if the rod moves westward

questions.explanation: Induced EMF is zero because flux linked with it remains constant.

questions.question_text: A bar-magnet of the pole-strength 2 Amp-m is kept in a magnetic field of induction 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -5 Wb/m 2 such that the axis of the magnet makes an angle 30° with the direction of the field. If the couple acting on the magnet is found to be 80 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 Nm, then the distance between the poles of the magnet is:
questions.question_html: A bar-magnet of the pole-strength 2 Amp-m is kept in a magnetic field of induction 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 Wb/m2 such that the axis of the magnet makes an angle 30° with the direction of the field. If the couple acting on the magnet is found to be 80 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 Nm, then the distance between the poles of the magnet is:
questions.explanation: {tex}\tau=q_{m} \times 2 l \times B \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} 2l = {tex}\frac{\tau}{q_{m} \times B \sin \theta}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{80 \times 10^{-7}}{2 \times 4 \times 10^{-5} \times \sin 30^{\circ}}{/tex} = 0.20 m = 20 cm

questions.question_text: Damping in galvanometers is based on
questions.question_html: Damping in galvanometers is based on

options.text: needle movement being opposed by rubber touching the needle lightly
options.html: needle movement being opposed by rubber touching the needle lightly

options.text: eddy currents that oppose the motion and bringing the coil to rest quickly
options.html: eddy currents that oppose the motion and bringing the coil to rest quickly

options.text: dash pot opposing the motion of the galvanometer needle
options.html: dash pot opposing the motion of the galvanometer needle

options.text: needle movement being opposed by oil
options.html: needle movement being opposed by oil

questions.correct_option_text: eddy currents that oppose the motion and bringing the coil to rest quickly
questions.explanation: The coil of galvanometer is wound on a light metal frame. When the coil and frame rotate in the field of the permanent magnet, the eddy current set up in the frame oppose the motion so that the coil returns to zero quickly.

questions.question_text: Mr. Iyer takes a grinder rated 220 V, 50 Hz to the U.S where supply is 110 V, 60 Hz. He needs to use a
questions.question_html: Mr. Iyer takes a grinder rated 220 V, 50 Hz to the U.S where supply is 110 V, 60 Hz. He needs to use a
questions.explanation: As, {tex}\frac{{{N_p}}}{{{N_s}}} = \frac{{{V_p}}}{{{V_s}}}\left( { = \frac{{input}}{{output}}} \right){/tex} Thus, {tex}\frac{{{N_p}}}{{{N_s}}} = \frac{{110}}{{220}} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} As, N p < N s , hence it is a step-up transformer.

questions.question_text: A proton is about 1840 times heavier than an electron. When it is accelerated by a potential difference of 1 kV, its kinetic energy will be:
questions.question_html: A proton is about 1840 times heavier than an electron. When it is accelerated by a potential difference of 1 kV, its kinetic energy will be:
questions.explanation: K.E. gained = qV = e {tex}\times{/tex} 1 kV = 1 keV

questions.question_text: Two cities are 150 km apart. Electric power is sent from one city to another city through copper wires. The fall of potential per km is 8 volt and the average resistance per km is 0.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . The power loss in the wires is:
questions.question_html: Two cities are 150 km apart. Electric power is sent from one city to another city through copper wires. The fall of potential per km is 8 volt and the average resistance per km is 0.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. The power loss in the wires is:
questions.explanation: Total resistance of the wires = 150 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = 75 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Current through the wires, l = {tex}\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta R}=\frac{8}{0.5}{/tex} = 16 A P = I 2 R = (16) 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 75 = 19200 W = 19.2 kW

questions.question_text: For a coil having L = 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 3 H, current flows at the rate of 10 -3 A/s. The emf induced is:
questions.question_html: For a coil having L = 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 103 H, current flows at the rate of 10-3 A/s. The emf induced is:
questions.explanation: {tex}e = L{{di} \over {dt}} = 2 \times {10^3} \times {10^{ - 3}} = 2{\rm{V}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Which of the following combinations should be selected for better tuning of an LCR circuit used for communication?
questions.question_html: Which of the following combinations should be selected for better tuning of an LCR circuit used for communication?
questions.explanation: Where R is the resistance, L is the inductance and C is the capacitance of the circuit. For high Q factor, R should be low, L should be high and C should be low. These conditions are best satisfied by the values given in option. Q= {tex}{1\over R} \sqrt {l\over c}{/tex}

questions.question_text: A point charge q = -8.0 nC is located at the origin. Magnitude of the electric field vector at the field point x = 0.949 m, y = -1.643 m is
questions.question_html: A point charge q = -8.0 nC is located at the origin. Magnitude of the electric field vector at the field point x = 0.949 m, y = -1.643 m is

questions.question_text: How much work is required to carry a 6 {tex}\mu{/tex} C charge from the negative to the positive terminal of a 9 V battery?
questions.question_html: How much work is required to carry a 6 {tex}\mu{/tex}C charge from the negative to the positive terminal of a 9 V battery?
questions.explanation: W = qV = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 C {tex}\times{/tex} 9 V = 54 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 J

questions.question_text: The cyclotron frequency of an electron gyrating in a magnetic field of 1 T is approximate:
questions.question_html: The cyclotron frequency of an electron gyrating in a magnetic field of 1 T is approximate:
questions.explanation: {tex}f_{c}=\frac{q B}{2 \pi m}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1}{2 \times 3.14 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31}} \mathrm{~Hz}{/tex} = 28 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 Hz = 28 GHz

questions.question_text: An inductor L having resistance R and a capacitor of capacitance C are connected to an alternating source of e.m.f. The quality factor of the circuit is
questions.question_html: An inductor L having resistance R and a capacitor of capacitance C are connected to an alternating source of e.m.f. The quality factor of the circuit is
questions.explanation: The quality factor of an LCR-circuit is given by {tex}Q =\frac{\omega_0 \ L}{R}{/tex}

Two similar magnets of magnetic moments M1 and M2 are taken and vibrate in a vibration magnetometer with their

  1. like poles together
  2. unlike poles together. If the ratio of the time periods is {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}, then the ratio of M1 and M2 {tex}\left(\text { i.e., } \frac{M_{1}}{M_{2}}\right){/tex} is

questions.question_text: The electrostatic potential on the surface of a charged conducting sphere is 100V. Two statments are made in this regard: S 1 : At any point inside the sphere, electric intensity is zero. S 2 : At any point inside the sphere, the electrostatic potential is 100V. Which of the following is a correct statement?
questions.question_html:
The electrostatic potential on the surface of a charged conducting sphere is 100V. Two statments are made in this regard:
S1 : At any point inside the sphere, electric intensity is zero.
S2 : At any point inside the sphere, the electrostatic potential is 100V.
Which of the following is a correct statement?

options.text: S 1 is true but S 2 is false.
options.html: S<sub>1</sub> is true but S<sub>2</sub> is false.

options.text: S 1 is true, S 2 is also true and S 1 is the cause of S 2 .
options.html: S<sub>1</sub> is true, S<sub>2</sub> is also true and S<sub>1</sub> is the cause of S<sub>2</sub>.

options.text: S 1 is true, S 2 is also true but the statements are independent.
options.html: S<sub>1</sub> is true, S<sub>2</sub> is also true but the statements are independent.

questions.correct_option_text: S 1 is true, S 2 is also true and S 1 is the cause of S 2 .
questions.explanation: A charged sphere doesnt enclosed any charge due to which electric field is zero inside the sphere. The relation between electric field intensity E and potential {tex}(\mathrm{V}) \text { is }\left[E=-\frac{d V}{d r}\right]{/tex} Here, E = 0 inside the sphere then {tex}\frac{d V}{d r}=0{/tex} i.e., V = constant. E = 0 inside charged sphere, the potential is constant or V = 100 Everywhere inside the sphere and it verifies the shielding effect also. Hence verifies the option

questions.question_text: A conducting sphere of radius 10 cm is charged with 10 μC. Another uncharged sphere of radius 20 cm is allowed to touch it for some time. After that if the spheres are separated, then surface density of charges on the spheres will be in the ratio of
questions.question_html: A conducting sphere of radius 10 cm is charged with 10 μC. Another uncharged sphere of radius 20 cm is allowed to touch it for some time. After that if the spheres are separated, then surface density of charges on the spheres will be in the ratio of
questions.explanation: When the two conducting spheres touch each other there will be a flow of charge until they both have the same potential. Let R 1 and R 2 be the radii of spheres 1 and 2, respectively. Let Q 1 and Q 2 be the charges on spheres 1 and 2, respectively, after they are separated. Let the common potential=V, {tex}{Q_1} = 4\pi { \in _o}{R_1}{/tex} {tex}{Q_2} = 4\pi { \in _o}{R_2}{/tex} R 1 = 10cm R 2 = 20cm Surface charge density on sphere 1, {tex}{\sigma _1} = \frac{{{Q_1}}}{{4\pi R_1^2}}{/tex} Surface charge density on sphere 2, {tex}{\sigma _2} = \frac{{{Q_2}}}{{4\pi R_2^2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{\sigma _1}}}{{{\sigma _2}}} = \frac{{{Q_1}}}{{{Q_2}}} \times \frac{{R_2^2}}{{R_1^2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{\sigma _1}}}{{{\sigma _2}}} = \frac{{{R_1}}}{{{R_2}}} \times \frac{{R_2^2}}{{R_1^2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{\sigma _1}}}{{{\sigma _2}}} = \frac{{{R_2}}}{{{R_1}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{\sigma _1}}}{{{\sigma _2}}} = \frac{{20}}{{10}}{/tex} {tex}{\sigma _1}:{\sigma _2} = 2:1{/tex}

questions.question_text: A transformer having efficiency of 90% is working on 200 V and 3 kW power supply. If the current in the secondary coil is 6 A, the voltage across the secondary coil and the current in the primary coil respectively are
questions.question_html: A transformer having efficiency of 90% is working on 200 V and 3 kW power supply. If the current in the secondary coil is 6 A, the voltage across the secondary coil and the current in the primary coil respectively are
questions.explanation: Power output = {tex}\frac{90}{100} \times{/tex} 3 kW = 2.7 kW I S = 6A {tex}V_{S}=\frac{2.7 \mathrm{~kW}}{6 \mathrm{~A}}=\frac{2700 \mathrm{~W}}{6 \mathrm{~A}}{/tex} = 450 W {tex}I_{P}=\frac{3 \mathrm{~kW}}{200 \mathrm{~V}}=\frac{3000 \mathrm{~W}}{200 \mathrm{~V}}{/tex} = 15 A

questions.question_text: If the polarity of induced emf is such that it tends to produce a current which aids the change in magnetic flux that produced it, which conservation law is violated?
questions.question_html: If the polarity of induced emf is such that it tends to produce a current which aids the change in magnetic flux that produced it, which conservation law is violated?
questions.explanation: In this case we would be obtaining electrical energy continuously without doing any work.

questions.question_text: If a bar magnet placed in a uniform magnetic filed of strength 0.3 T, with its axis 30 o to a uniform magnetic field, experiences a torque of 0.06 Nm. then the magnetic moment of bar magnet is
questions.question_html: If a bar magnet placed in a uniform magnetic filed of strength 0.3 T, with its axis 30o to a uniform magnetic field, experiences a torque of 0.06 Nm. then the magnetic moment of bar magnet is
questions.explanation: {tex}\begin{array}{l}{\text { Here, } B=0 \cdot 3 T, \theta=30^{\circ}, \tau=0 \cdot 06 N-m, M=?} \\ {\text { As } \tau=M b \sin \theta, M=\frac{\tau}{B \sin \theta}=\frac{0 \cdot 06}{0 \cdot 3 \sin 30^{\circ}}=0.4\;Am^2}\end{array} {/tex}

questions.question_text: A beam of cathode rays is subjected to crossed electric (E) and magnetic fields (B). The fields are adjusted such that the beam is not deflected. The specific charge of the cathode rays is given by where V is the potential difference between cathode and anode.
questions.question_html: A beam of cathode rays is subjected to crossed electric (E) and magnetic fields (B). The fields are adjusted such that the beam is not deflected. The specific charge of the cathode rays is given by where V is the potential difference between cathode and anode.
questions.explanation: For undeflected beam, {tex}v=\frac{E}{B}{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=e V{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad v^{2}=\frac{2 e V}{m}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{2 e V}{m}=\frac{E^{2}}{B^{2}} {/tex} or {tex} \frac{e}{m}=\frac{E^{2}}{2 V B^{2}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: A uniform electric field and a uniform magnetic field are produced, pointed in the same direction. When an electron is projected with its velocity pointed in the same direction,
questions.question_html: A uniform electric field and a uniform magnetic field are produced, pointed in the same direction. When an electron is projected with its velocity pointed in the same direction,

options.text: the electron will turn to its left
options.html: the electron will turn to its left

options.text: the electron velocity will increase in magnitude
options.html: the electron velocity will increase in magnitude

options.text: the electron will turn to its right
options.html: the electron will turn to its right

options.text: the electron velocity will decrease in magnitude
options.html: the electron velocity will decrease in magnitude

questions.correct_option_text: the electron velocity will decrease in magnitude
questions.explanation: The force experienced by an electron in a combined action of magnetic and electric fields is {tex}\vec F = - e\left( {\vec v \times \vec B + \vec E} \right){/tex} . Since the electron moves in the same direction of the magnetic field, it experiences no force due to the magnetic field. {tex} - e\left( {\vec v \times \vec B} \right) = 0{/tex} . The electron is not deflected from its straight line path. The total force on the electron, {tex}\vec F = - e\left( {\vec E} \right){/tex} . It experiences a force opposite to the direction of the electric field and to its direction of motion. The electron suffers retardation and its velocity decreases.

questions.question_text: The angle between the electric lines of force and an equipotential surface:
questions.question_html: The angle between the electric lines of force and an equipotential surface:
questions.explanation: An electric line of force is perpendicular to the equipotential surface at every point.

questions.question_text: A tennis ball which has been covered with charges is suspended by a thread so that it hangs between two metal plates. One plate is earthed, while other is attracted to a high voltage generator. The ball
questions.question_html: A tennis ball which has been covered with charges is suspended by a thread so that it hangs between two metal plates. One plate is earthed, while other is attracted to a high voltage generator. The ball

options.text: swings backward & forward hitting each plate in turn
options.html: swings backward & forward hitting each plate in turn

options.text: is repelled by earthed plate and stays there
options.html: is repelled by earthed plate and stays there

options.text: is attracted to the high voltage plate and stays there
options.html: is attracted to the high voltage plate and stays there

questions.correct_option_text: swings backward & forward hitting each plate in turn
questions.explanation: When the other plate is connected to the high voltage generator, the negative charge induced on the ball cause attraction. When it strikes the +ve plate charge distribution again takes place. This causes repulsion. Hence, the ball swings backwards and forward hitting each plate in turn. Or it is attracted by the high voltage plate, when charge is shared, ball is repelled until it goes to other plate and whole of the charge is transferred to the earth and the process is repeated.

questions.question_text: An ac voltage is applied to a resistance R and an inductor L in series. If R and the inductive reactance are both equal to 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , the phase difference between the applied voltage and the current in the circuit is:
questions.question_html: An ac voltage is applied to a resistance R and an inductor L in series. If R and the inductive reactance are both equal to 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex}, the phase difference between the applied voltage and the current in the circuit is:
questions.explanation: Phase difference, {tex}\phi=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega L}{R}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{3}\right)=\tan ^{-1}(1)=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}

questions.question_text: A long solenoid has 1000 turns. When a current of 4 A flows through it, the magnetic flux linked with each turn of the solenoid is 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 Wb. The self-inductance of the solenoid is:
questions.question_html: A long solenoid has 1000 turns. When a current of 4 A flows through it, the magnetic flux linked with each turn of the solenoid is 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 Wb. The self-inductance of the solenoid is:
questions.explanation: N = 100, I = 4A, {tex}\phi=4 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~Wb}{/tex} {tex}\phi N = LI{/tex} {tex}\therefore L=\frac{\phi N}{I}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000}{4} \mathrm{H}{/tex} = 1 H

questions.question_text: Magnetic dipole moment is a vector quantity directed from
questions.question_html: Magnetic dipole moment is a vector quantity directed from
questions.explanation: Magnetic dipole & dipole moment A magnetic N and S pole make up a magnetic dipole Magnetic dipole moment is analogous to an electric dipole moment. Direction Vector from S to N pole (by convention).

Current {tex}{{\rm{i}}_{\rm{1}}}{/tex} in coil {tex}{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{1}}}{/tex} is changing with time. The emf in {tex}{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{2}}}{/tex} is given by

questions.question_text: If the distance between parallel plates of a capacitor is halved and dielectric constant is doubled then the capacitance:
questions.question_html: If the distance between parallel plates of a capacitor is halved and dielectric constant is doubled then the capacitance:
questions.explanation: {tex}C=\kappa C_{0}=\kappa \frac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d}{/tex} i.e {tex}C \propto \frac{\kappa}{d}{/tex} {tex}\therefore C \propto \frac{2 \kappa}{\frac d 2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}C^{\prime} \propto \frac{4 k}{d}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{C^{\prime}}{C}=4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C' = 4 C

questions.question_text: The heat produced by 100 W heater in 2 minutes is equal to:
questions.question_html: The heat produced by 100 W heater in 2 minutes is equal to:
questions.explanation: Heat produced = P {tex}\times{/tex} t = 100 W {tex}\times{/tex} 60s = 12000 J = {tex}\frac{12000}{4.2 \times 1000} \text { kcal }{/tex} = 2.8 kcal

questions.question_text: The primary of step-down transformer used for ringing doorbell has 2000 turns of fine wire and the secondary has 100 turns. This transformer when connected to a 110 V A.C. source will deliver at its secondary a potential difference of:
questions.question_html: The primary of step-down transformer used for ringing doorbell has 2000 turns of fine wire and the secondary has 100 turns. This transformer when connected to a 110 V A.C. source will deliver at its secondary a potential difference of:
questions.explanation: {tex}V_{s}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}} \times V_{p}=\frac{100}{2000} \times 110{/tex} = 5.5 V

questions.question_text: A jet plane is travelling towards west at a speed of 1800 km/h. If the Earth’s magnetic field at the location has a magnitude of {tex}{\rm{5 }} \times {\rm{ 1}}0 ^{- {\rm{4} }}T{/tex} and the dip angle is {tex}{\rm{3}}0^\circ {/tex} , voltage difference developed between the ends of the wing having a span of 25 m is approximately
questions.question_html: A jet plane is travelling towards west at a speed of 1800 km/h. If the Earth’s magnetic field at the location has a magnitude of {tex}{\rm{5 }} \times {\rm{ 1}}0 ^{- {\rm{4} }}T{/tex} and the dip angle is {tex}{\rm{3}}0^\circ {/tex}, voltage difference developed between the ends of the wing having a span of 25 m is approximately
questions.explanation: {tex}e = {(B\sin \phi )vl = 5 \times {{10}^{ - 4}} \times {1 \over 2}} \times 500 \times 25 = 3.1{\rm{V}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: When iron filings are sprinkled on a sheet of glass placed over a short bar magnet then, the iron filings form a pattern suggesting that the magnet has
questions.question_html: When iron filings are sprinkled on a sheet of glass placed over a short bar magnet then, the iron filings form a pattern suggesting that the magnet has

options.text: north pole similar to the positive charge
options.html: north pole similar to the positive charge

options.text: south pole similar to the negative charge
options.html: south pole similar to the negative charge

options.text: two poles similar to the positive and negative charge of an electric dipole
options.html: two poles similar to the positive and negative charge of an electric dipole

questions.correct_option_text: two poles similar to the positive and negative charge of an electric dipole
questions.explanation: North pole act as positive end and South pole act as negative end. The iron fillings in the presence of magnetic field gets magnetized and form a tiny magnet which then gets attracted to the poles of bar magnet. North pole of bar magnet attracts south pole of tiny magnet and vice-versa. So all the iron fillings are arranged as the magnetic field lines of bar magnet.

questions.question_text: The internal resistance of the primary cell is 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . It generates a current of 0.2 A in an external resistance of 21 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . The rate at which chemical energy is consumed which is providing the current is:
questions.question_html: The internal resistance of the primary cell is 4 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. It generates a current of 0.2 A in an external resistance of 21 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. The rate at which chemical energy is consumed which is providing the current is:
questions.explanation: Rate of consumption of chemical energy = Rate of production of heat = I 2 (R + r) = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} (21 + 4) J/s = 1 J/s

questions.question_text: Three point charges +q, -2q and +q are placed at points (x = 0, y = a, z = 0), (x = 0, y = 0, z = 0) and (x = a, y = 0, z = 0) and (x = a, y = 0, z = 0) respectively. The magnitude and direction of the electric dipole moment vector of this charge assembly are
questions.question_html: Three point charges +q, -2q and +q are placed at points (x = 0, y = a, z = 0), (x = 0, y = 0, z = 0) and (x = a, y = 0, z = 0) and (x = a, y = 0, z = 0) respectively. The magnitude and direction of the electric dipole moment vector of this charge assembly are

options.text: qa along the line joining the points (x = 0, y = 0, z = 0) and (x = a, y = a, z = 0)
options.html: qa along the line joining the points (x = 0, y = 0, z = 0) and (x = a, y = a, z = 0)

options.text: {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} qa along the line joining the points (x = 0, y = 0, z = 0) and (x = a, y = a, z = 0)
options.html: {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} qa along the line joining the points (x = 0, y = 0, z = 0) and (x = a, y = a, z = 0)

questions.correct_option_text: {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} qa along the line joining the points (x = 0, y = 0, z = 0) and (x = a, y = a, z = 0)
questions.explanation: The given charge assembly is equivalent to two dipoles. One dipole of charges -q and +q has dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}_{1}{/tex} along +ve X-direction and other dipole of charges -q and +q has dipole moment {tex}\overrightarrow{p_{2}}{/tex} along +ve Y-direction. The resultant dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} has magnitude, p = {tex}\sqrt{p_{1}^{2}+p_{2}^{2}}=\sqrt{q^{2} a^{2}+q^{2} a^{2}}=\sqrt{2} q a{/tex} {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} is directed along {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}}{/tex} , where P is (a, a, 0).

questions.question_text: The deflection in moving coil galvanometer falls from 50 divisions to 10 divisions, when a shunt of {tex}12\Omega{/tex} is applied, the resistance of galvanometer coil is
questions.question_html: The deflection in moving coil galvanometer falls from 50 divisions to 10 divisions, when a shunt of {tex}12\Omega{/tex} is applied, the resistance of galvanometer coil is
questions.explanation: If I is current in galvanometer for unit division, then voltage across galvanometer and shunt are equal. We know that, {tex}\frac{I_{G}}{I_{s}}=\frac{S}{G}{/tex} So, {tex}G=\left(\frac{I_{s}}{I_{G}}\right)(S){/tex} {tex}\begin{array}{l}{=\left(\frac{50-10}{10}\right)(12)} \\ {=48 \Omega}\end{array}{/tex} Thus, G = {tex}48\Omega{/tex}

questions.question_text: The force between two magnetic poles is F. If the distance between the poles and pole strengths of each pole are doubled, then the force experienced is:
questions.question_html: The force between two magnetic poles is F. If the distance between the poles and pole strengths of each pole are doubled, then the force experienced is:
questions.explanation: {tex}F\alpha {q_mq_m'\over r^2}{/tex} Hence {tex}\frac{{F'}}{F} = ({2q_m2q_m'\over 4r^2})/{q_mq_m'\over r^2}=1{/tex} or F' = F

questions.question_text: Assertion (A): Three equal charges are situated on a circle of radius r such that they form an equilateral triangle, then the electric field intensity at the center is zero. Reason (R): The force on the unit positive charge at the center, due to the three equal charges is represented by the three sides of a triangle taken in the same order. Therefore, the electric field intensity at the center is zero.
questions.question_html:
Assertion (A): Three equal charges are situated on a circle of radius r such that they form an equilateral triangle, then the electric field intensity at the center is zero.
Reason (R): The force on the unit positive charge at the center, due to the three equal charges is represented by the three sides of a triangle taken in the same order. Therefore, the electric field intensity at the center is zero.

questions.explanation: Electromagnets are magnets, which can be turned on and off by switching the current on and off. As the material in electromagnets is subjected to cyclic change (magnification and demagnetisation), the hysteresis loss of the material must be small. The material should attain high value of I and B with low value of magnetising field intensity H. As soft iron has small coercivity, so it is a best choice for this purpose.

questions.question_text: An electric dipole coincides on Z-axis and its midpoint is on the origin of the coordinate system. questions.question_html: An electric dipole coincides on Z-axis and its midpoint is on the origin of the coordinate system. The electric field at an axial point at a distance z from the origin is Ez and the electric field at an equatorial point at a distance y from the origin is Ey. Here z = y >> {tex}\alpha{/tex}, so {tex}\frac{\left|E_{z}\right|}{\left|E_{y}\right|}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: For z >> a, |E z | = {tex}\frac{2 p}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} z^{3}}{/tex} For y >> a, |E y | = {tex}\frac{p}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} y^{3}}{/tex} For z = y >> a, {tex}\frac{\left|E_{z}\right|}{\left|E_{y}\right|}{/tex} = 2

questions.question_text: Instructions for Question 52 to 55: In 1942, a German physicist Kirchhoff extended Ohm's law to complicated circuits and gave two laws, which enable us to determine current in any part of such a circuit. According to Kirchhoff's first rule, the algebraic sum of the currents meeting at a junction in a closed electric circuit is zero. The current flowing in a conductor towards the junction is taken as positive and the current flowing away from the junction is taken as negative. According to Kirchhoff's second rule, in a closed loop, the algebraic sum of the emf's and algebraic sum of the products of current and resistance in the various arms of the loop is zero. While traversing loop, if negative pole of the cell is encountered first, then its emf is negative, otherwise positive. Kirchhoff's 1st law follows
questions.question_html:
Instructions for Question 52 to 55:

In 1942, a German physicist Kirchhoff extended Ohm's law to complicated circuits and gave two laws, which enable us to determine current in any part of such a circuit. According to Kirchhoff's first rule, the algebraic sum of the currents meeting at a junction in a closed electric circuit is zero. The current flowing in a conductor towards the junction is taken as positive and the current flowing away from the junction is taken as negative. According to Kirchhoff's second rule, in a closed loop, the algebraic sum of the emf's and algebraic sum of the products of current and resistance in the various arms of the loop is zero. While traversing loop, if negative pole of the cell is encountered first, then its emf is negative, otherwise positive.


Kirchhoff's 1st law follows
questions.explanation: Kirchhoff's 1 st law is based on law of conservation of charge whereas Kirchhoff's II nd law is based on law of conservation of energy.
